name: Platform Neutrality Advocacy
consequences: Immediate: Formal policy brief reduces reliance on Apple/Google signals → Systemic: 30% faster procurement cycles for platform‑neutral solutions → Strategic: Enhances national resilience while risking increased vendor negotiations and implications for agencies.
options: Submit a formal policy brief to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen recommending incremental platform‑neutral clauses in upcoming procurement documents.
Launch a public awareness campaign highlighting risks of platform lock‑in, coupled with targeted stakeholder workshops to build consensus on platform‑neutral requirements.

Create a joint venture with a European open‑source identity consortium to develop a certified fallback authentication framework, deploying it as a plug‑in for AltID and offering it as a commercial service to public agencies.
review: Controls Speed vs. Vendor Negotiations. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of maintaining an independent certification process for fallback authentication.

name: Regulatory Procurement Leverage
consequences: Immediate: Introduce mandatory supplier diversity clauses in AltID tenders → Systemic: 25% reduction in single‑vendor risk exposure → Strategic: Strengthens sovereignty but may increase procurement lead times and compliance costs for agencies.
options: Advocate for a single clause mandating that no more than 50% of authentication services come from any one foreign vendor.
Propose a phased procurement framework that requires proof of platform‑neutral fallback capability before final award.

Design a blockchain‑based decentralized identity marketplace that allows public agencies to source authentication services directly from vetted, sovereign‑hosted providers, bypassing traditional tender processes.
review: Controls Vendor Concentration vs. Procurement Efficiency. Weakness: The options ignore the legal constraints around non‑EU vendor participation in public procurement.

name: Technical Demonstrator Credibility
consequences: Immediate: Build two non‑production demonstrators showcasing fallback authentication → Systemic: 20% increase in stakeholder confidence → Strategic: Accelerates policy adoption while potentially diverting resources from broader research and for maintain long‑term support.
options: Develop a single mobile‑only demonstrator using FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS, limited to internal testing.
Deploy two demonstrators—mobile and browser‑based—validated by an external security audit, to showcase fallback paths.
Create a live, cross‑platform, cloud‑hosted demo that integrates hardware‑token authentication with a zero‑trust network, enabling real‑time testing by government agencies.
review: Controls Demonstrator Scope vs. Resource Allocation. Weakness: The options fail to account for the need to secure long‑term funding for ongoing demonstration maintenance.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Dynamics
consequences: Immediate: Expand coalition to include privacy NGOs and academia → Systemic: 15% faster consensus on policy language → Strategic: Builds political momentum but may dilute focus on technical depth and stakeholder engagement.
options: Maintain current coalition of civil‑society and privacy advocates, focusing on policy briefs.
Add academic researchers and security experts to the coalition, organizing quarterly joint workshops.
Form a transnational coalition with EU member states facing similar platform lock‑in, establishing a shared lobbying platform and joint research grants.
review: Controls Coalition Breadth vs. Technical Focus. Weakness: The options overlook the challenge of aligning diverse stakeholder priorities within a single advocacy agenda.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Engage with EU Digital Identity Wallet WG → Systemic: 10% faster alignment of Danish requirements with EU standards → Strategic: Enhances export potential but may increase regulatory complexity and compliance monitoring.
options: Submit a formal comment to the EU Digital Identity Wallet WG on platform‑neutral language.
Participate in EU working groups, proposing concrete fallback authentication standards for eIDAS2 wallets.
Lead an EU‑wide initiative to develop a new open‑source identity protocol, backed by a consortium of sovereign states, to replace existing eIDAS2 specifications.
review: Controls EU Alignment vs. Domestic Implementation Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of EU standard delays on national procurement timelines.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Lever
consequences: Immediate: Mobilises diverse civil society and policy actors → Systemic: 30% faster stakeholder alignment through joint advocacy → Strategic: Enables policy leverage to shift procurement priorities.
options: Build a formal advisory council of existing privacy NGOs and academia.
Launch a cross‑sector working group with civil society, industry, and government representatives.
Create a decentralized autonomous organization (DAO) that governs platform‑neutral identity standards and funds research.
review: Controls depth of engagement vs. speed of coalition formation. Weakness: The options fail to consider the entrenched bureaucratic inertia of government procurement bodies.

name: Technical Demonstration Lever
consequences: Immediate: Produces proof‑of‑concept fallback authentication → Systemic: 25% faster validation of standards compliance → Strategic: Provides evidence to counter vendor lock‑in arguments.
options: Develop a single mobile app using FIDO2 on GrapheneOS.
Build two demonstrators: a mobile app and a browser‑based fallback.
Deploy a blockchain‑based decentralized identity wallet that operates independently of OS integrity signals.
review: Controls technical depth vs. resource allocation. Weakness: The options fail to consider scalability to national deployment.

name: Policy Framing Lever
consequences: Immediate: Reframes dependency risk as national security threat → Systemic: 20% increase in policy support for platform neutrality → Strategic: Shifts legislative agenda toward procurement neutrality.
options: Publish a risk assessment report highlighting supplier concentration.
Submit a formal policy brief to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen advocating for platform‑neutral clauses.
Draft a new national digital sovereignty law mandating fallback authentication pathways.
review: Controls narrative framing vs. risk of politicisation. Weakness: The options fail to consider privacy trade‑offs inherent in platform‑neutral mandates.

name: Procurement Language Lever
consequences: Immediate: Introduces platform‑neutral clauses into procurement documents → Systemic: 15% reduction in vendor lock‑in risk → Strategic: Sets precedent for future identity procurement.
options: Add a clause requiring alternative authentication methods.
Specify that procurement must include fallback authentication options.
Mandate that all identity solutions must be certified on a public blockchain platform.
review: Controls specificity of procurement language vs. flexibility for vendors. Weakness: The options fail to consider implementation complexity for existing vendors.

name: EU Standards Alignment Lever
consequences: Immediate: Aligns Danish proposals with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards → Systemic: 10% faster harmonisation of cross‑border identity protocols → Strategic: Positions Denmark as a leader in platform‑neutral identity.
options: Submit technical comments to the EU working group.
Co‑author an EU white paper on fallback authentication.
Propose a new EU directive establishing mandatory fallback authentication for all member states.
review: Controls national autonomy vs. EU integration. Weakness: The options fail to consider national legal constraints on mandatory fallback authentication.

name: Policy Framing Lever
consequences: Immediate: reframing dependency risk as national security threat → Systemic: 30% faster stakeholder alignment through urgency messaging → Strategic: increased likelihood of procurement policy shift.
options: Emphasize existing legal frameworks and incremental risk mitigation
Introduce a formal risk assessment mandate for all digital identity procurements
Launch a public referendum on digital sovereignty to force policy change
review: Controls Speed vs. Thoroughness. Weakness: The options fail to consider long‑term public trust erosion.

name: Coalition Building Lever
consequences: Immediate: broaden stakeholder base → Systemic: 25% faster consensus building through cross‑sector networks → Strategic: higher resilience to political pushback.
options: Engage only with established civil society NGOs already involved in digital rights
Expand coalition to include academic researchers and security firms
Create a transnational digital sovereignty alliance with other Nordic countries to amplify pressure
review: Controls Breadth vs. Depth. Weakness: The options fail to consider resource constraints of small NGOs.

name: Technical Demonstration Lever
consequences: Immediate: produce credible proof‑of‑concept → Systemic: 20% faster technical validation by regulators → Strategic: increased credibility for policy proposals.
options: Build a single mobile‑based FIDO2 demonstrator on GrapheneOS
Develop both mobile and browser‑based fallback demonstrators using open standards
Deploy a distributed ledger‑based identity verification prototype that operates independently of any OS
review: Controls Depth vs. Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider scalability of demonstrator to production.

name: Procurement Language Lever
consequences: Immediate: embed platform‑neutral clauses in tender documents → Systemic: 15% reduction in vendor lock‑in risk → Strategic: long‑term cost savings and resilience.
options: Add a clause requiring vendor to provide platform‑neutral APIs
Mandate that all identity solutions must support at least two independent OS platforms
Require that all procurement include a mandatory fallback authentication path using hardware tokens or browser‑based solutions
review: Controls Specificity vs. Flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider procurement cycle length.

name: EU Standards Alignment Lever
consequences: Immediate: align Danish proposals with EU Digital Identity Wallet standards → Systemic: 10% faster harmonization across member states → Strategic: stronger bargaining power in EU policy forums.
options: Submit technical comments to EU working groups
Propose amendments to EU Digital Identity Wallet specifications that include platform‑neutral fallback
Lead a pan‑European initiative to create a new EU standard for platform‑neutral identity wallets
review: Controls Sovereignty vs. Integration. Weakness: The options fail to consider national legal constraints on EU directives.
